topic: two people getting to know each other

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: Hi! I'm Mara. I don't think we've met — what's your name?

transcript.speaker: B
transcript.text: Hey Mara! I'm Diego. I work in city planning — nothing too glamorous, just trying to make sure traffic flows and zoning makes sense. Grew up near the coast, so I've always got half a mind to be near the water. How about you — what do you do?

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: Nice to meet you, Diego! I work in library services — I love being surrounded by books and helping people find exactly what they're looking for. I grew up in the mountains, so I crave that quiet, crisp air, but I can totally see the appeal of the coast. Do you get to enjoy the water much where you live now, or is it more of a "someday" thing?

transcript.speaker: B
transcript.text: Honestly, it's more of a "someday" thing right now — I live inland, so the beach is a weekend trip at best. But I make it happen a few times a year, and honestly, that's enough to keep me going. Do you get back to the mountains much, or is city life your thing now?

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: City life has grown on me, honestly — there's always something happening, people to meet. But I do miss those mountains. I try to visit my folks a couple times a year, and it's like a reset button every time. A few times a year for the coast, a few times a year for the mountains... sounds like we're both making it work! So besides chasing water and dodging traffic complaints, what do you like to do when you're not solving the puzzle of urban life?

transcript.speaker: B
transcript.text: Ha, you said it — urban puzzles are my specialty. When I'm not working, I like to cook, nothing fancy, just experimenting with new recipes. Weekends usually involve a long run or a hike if I can manage it. What about you — any hobbies beyond the book world?

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: Cooking and running — I love that balance of creating something and then burning it off! I do some pottery on the side, which is funny because it's basically the opposite of city planning — no grids, no logic, just hands in clay. Very therapeutic. Do you have a signature dish you're always proud of, or are you more of a "throw it together and hope for the best" kind of cook?

transcript.speaker: B
transcript.text: I'm definitely more of a "throw it together" person, but my pasta sauce has become sort of legendary among friends — lots of garlic, red wine, and patience. Took a while to get it right, but now I can't mess it up. Pottery sounds amazing though — what's your favorite thing you've made?

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: Oh, the legendary pasta sauce — I respect the patience factor! My favorite piece is probably this wonky, lopsided vase that I made completely by accident. It's got this weird little tilt to it, but I love it because it's the first piece that actually felt like *me*. My mom claimed it, so now it lives on her windowsill in the mountains. Do you have any cookbooks you swear by, or do you just improvise with whatever's in the fridge?

transcript.speaker: B
transcript.text: Honestly, I improvise most of the time, but I do keep an old copy of Joy of Cooking my grandma gave me — more for nostalgia than anything. Sometimes I'll flip through it for inspiration, but half the fun is just seeing what I've got and building from there. Do you have a favorite cookbook, or does pottery have its own library of technique books?

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: The library has a whole collection of pottery books I've basically memorized at this point — but for my own shelf, nothing beats a good old-fashioned recipe collection. My sister's always giving me Italian family recipes she's hoarded, so I've got this worn folder stuffed with scribbled cards. There's something about those handwritten ones, isn't there? Like your grandma's Joy of Cooking — that's the real stuff. Do you have any of her recipes tucked in there, or is it more of a general reference thing?

transcript.speaker: B
transcript.text: There's a few tucked in there — her lemon cake recipe is my favorite. She always wrote notes in the margins, like "don't overmix!" or "this feeds a crowd." Makes me smile every time. That worn folder of your sister's recipes sounds like a treasure though.

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: That's so sweet — those margin notes are the best part, honestly. "This feeds a crowd" might be my favorite kind of kitchen wisdom. I bet you make that lemon cake for special occasions, right? Or do you resist the urge to mess with the original?

transcript.speaker: B
transcript.text: Oh, special occasions only — and I do stick pretty close to the original. Maybe I'll swap in a little lavender if I'm feeling bold, but her version is hard to beat. Does your family have any holiday traditions centered around food?

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: Every year my mom insists on making this enormous batch of her homemade gnocchi from scratch — it's a whole ordeal, flour everywhere, everyone pitching in. She pretends it's a hassle but I think she loves it. I actually inherited her gnocchi board last Christmas, so now the tradition has officially passed to me. That's a lot of pressure, honestly! Does your family have any traditions you've quietly inherited — or maybe inherited reluctantly?

transcript.speaker: B
transcript.text: That's such a nice passing of the torch — pressure and all. My family tradition is less formal, but every Thanksgiving my dad insists on making his dry brined turkey, and no one is allowed to complain about it. He looks up the method every single year like it's his first time. I inherited the "not complaining" role, so that's mine to carry on.

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: Ha! "The not-complaining role" — that's the most dedicated kind of inheritance. I love that he commits to the process every single time, though. It's like he rediscovering it fresh each year. So between your dad's turkey method and your grandma's lemon cake, you've got quite the lineage of recipes going. Does any of that culinary wisdom rub off on your day-to-day cooking, or do you keep the experiments completely separate?

transcript.speaker: B
transcript.text: A little bit of both, honestly. The basics stick — don't rush a sauce, taste as you go — but my weeknight stuff is pretty loose. Weekends are when I play around more. Feels like the family recipes are more like anchors, you know? Something solid to come back to.

transcript.speaker: A
transcript.text: I totally get that — anchors are important, especially when you're experimenting. I think pottery's the same way for me: I have to learn the fundamentals before I can break the rules and make something wonky. Speaking of anchoring, is your dad still doing the turkey quest every year, or has anyone gently suggested he might... already have this down?
